explanation: except Luggage which would go to OBJECTS-LUGGAGE.

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.

A bag is a soft-material handling scene: cloth, leather, plastic or paper rustle plus a few dull thumps. Which material it is may be measurable; that it is a BAG rather than a suitcase or a purse is not.
UCS rule: anything that is Luggage goes to OBJECTS-LUGGAGE, not here — a purely lexical split.

explanation: sounds relating to these. Handling particularly. Some overlap or confusion with PAPER-HANDLE.

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.

The one semantic_only OBJECTS subcategory with a real acoustic profile: page-turn crackle, which IS PAPER-HANDLE (bright, dense micro-onsets, no ring). UCS itself admits the overlap. DSP can say 'this is paper'; it cannot say 'this is a book'.
A dropped hardcover adds a dull mid/low thump, and that thump is indistinguishable from any other object drop.

discriminators: NOT hopeless, unlike its OBJECTS siblings: coins are small METAL plates. The jingle signature is a bright, inharmonic, multi-onset ring — high spectral_centroid, high inharmonicity, a real decay tail, and transient_count well above one. DSP can narrow to 'small bright ringing metal'.
It cannot finish the job: OBJECTS-KEYS is the same physics (as are OBJECTS-JEWELRY and CHAINS). The coin-vs-keys call is lexical.

Against the soft materials this is unmistakable: PLASTIC, RUBBER, CLOTH, LEATHER and PAPER all have decay_time_seconds_60db an order of magnitude shorter and much lower centroids. Against METAL-IMPACT proper: coins are small, so the ring is shorter and far brighter.

This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.
Boxes, tupperware, lids opening and closing. The material (cardboard vs plastic vs glass) is somewhat measurable; that the object is a CONTAINER is not. Expect a lid click plus a body rattle.
Massively overlaps OBJECTS-PACKAGING, PLASTIC-HANDLE and PAPER-HANDLE.

explanation: etc. Some overlap possible with CLOTH categories. See also OBJECTS-JEWELRY. And OBJECTS-BAG.

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.

Shoes, belts, clothes, makeup. Nothing links these acoustically — they are cloth rustle, leather creak, plastic clicks or small taps depending on the item. UCS explicitly flags overlap with the CLOTH categories, OBJECTS-JEWELRY and OBJECTS-BAG.

explanation: things like that. Wood chair creaks could go in WOOD-FRICTION.

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.

Chair and couch movement: a scrape or drag plus upholstery rustle and body creaks. UCS rule mined from the explanation: a wood chair CREAK belongs in WOOD-FRICTION, not here — so a strongly pitched stick-slip creak should be handed to the WOOD sibling.
Otherwise indistinguishable from any other large-object drag.

explanation: things that you might find and use in a garden. See also TOOLS-GARDEN and MACHINES-GARDEN.

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.

Flower pots, bird feeders, watering cans: ceramic, metal and plastic objects with nothing acoustic in common. UCS cross-references TOOLS-GARDEN and MACHINES-GARDEN — the split is by object type, not by sound.

explanation: Objects found in a fitness center or school gym

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.
Dumbbells, mats, ropes, medicine balls. A dumbbell is METAL-IMPACT physics; a yoga mat is RUBBER-MOVEMENT physics. The category is a location, not a sound. UCS cross-references MACHINES-GYM.

explanation: things around the house that don't fit somewhere else.

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.
The residual bin for domestic objects — cleaning products, hangers, towel racks. Defined by what it is NOT, so no acoustic prior can be honest beyond 'a handled object'.

This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.

Bracelets, necklaces, rings are often small ringing metal, which puts a genuinely bright, inharmonic, multi-onset file in the same place as OBJECTS-COIN and OBJECTS-KEYS. But jewelry is also beads, leather cord and fabric, so the acoustic prior is not reliable enough to lift the tier the way it is for COIN and KEYS.
If a file really does read as bright ringing metal, treat COIN / KEYS / JEWELRY as one shortlist and let the synonyms pick.

discriminators: Like OBJECTS-COIN this is small ringing metal, and therefore signal_narrowable rather than semantic_only: a jingle is a dense, bright, inharmonic multi-onset cascade. A shaken keyring produces a markedly higher onset_rate than a coin drop — that is the only weak DSP lever between them, and it is not enough to commit on.
Keys-in-a-lock is a different scene: a few sharp metallic clicks with a short ring. Both readings stay inside the metal cluster.
Ruled out against every soft material by decay_time_seconds_60db and spectral_centroid: no plastic, rubber, cloth, leather or paper event rings this long this brightly.

This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.

Suitcases: zippers, wheels, handles, thumps. Each of those is a different morphology, and each is claimed by a better-specified subcategory — a suitcase zipper is OBJECTS-ZIPPER's signature, a rolling suitcase is OBJECTS-WHEELED's. LUGGAGE itself is purely lexical.
UCS rule: OBJECTS-BAG explicitly excludes luggage, so the two are separated by the word, not the waveform.

explanation: Common objects in hospital or medical office.

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.
Objects in a hospital or medical office — gloves, syringes, pill bottles. Latex glove snaps are RUBBER physics; pill bottles are PLASTIC physics. The category is a setting.

explanation: Object sounds not fitting another category in this list.

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.

The residual of a residual. Emitted by abstention whenever the OBJECTS siblings tie — which, given that almost the whole category is semantic_only, is the expected outcome for any un-named object-handling file.

explanation: paper cutter etc. Not machines like a printer which would go in MACHINES-OFFICE.

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.
Staplers, hole punches, paper cutters, scissors: small sharp metal-and-plastic clicks over paper rustle. UCS rule: a printer is a MACHINE, not an OBJECT.
A stapler click is a bright short impact, indistinguishable from a hundred other small mechanisms.

explanation: Items commonly used in packaging or packing

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.

Bubblewrap, styrofoam, labels, food packaging. Bubblewrap pops are a distinctive dense impulsive train and styrofoam squeaks are a bright friction — but both morphologies are already owned by PLASTIC, and 'packaging' itself is a use, not a sound.
Heavily overlaps PLASTIC-MOVEMENT, PLASTIC-HANDLE, PAPER-HANDLE (cardboard) and OBJECTS-CONTAINER.

explanation: Tape and other tacky sticky type sounds as well. Velcro probably better into CLOTH-RIP as that matches the 'sound' better.

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.

Physically this is friction — a sustained, low-crest, bright rasp as adhesive peels — so the morphology is real even though the object identity is not measurable. It looks almost exactly like PAPER-RIP and CLOTH-RIP.
UCS rule mined from the explanation: Velcro belongs in CLOTH-RIP, not here.
A tape-gun pull has a rising buzz that can approach OBJECTS-ZIPPER, but its onset_periodicity stays far lower.

explanation: Umbrella opens and closes. Not for rain on umbrella

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.

An umbrella open/close is a real composite event — a metal ratchet click, a spring pop, and a nylon canopy whoosh — but every component belongs to another subcategory and no single feature says 'umbrella'.
UCS rule mined from the explanation: rain ON an umbrella is RAIN-CLOTH, not here. That is a hard semantic redirect, not an acoustic one.

explanation: Non-motorized wheeled carts and dollies. Shopping cart

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.

Physically the only sustained-noise object here: a cart, dolly, stroller or wheelchair rolling produces a continuous rumble-and-caster-rattle bed, often many seconds long, with high stationarity and a low crest factor. That much is measurable.
The identity is not: a shopping cart, a gurney and a baggage cart are the same physics. And a stopped cart being handled collapses back into the generic handling scene.
Its long, stationary, sustained character is what separates it from every other OBJECTS subcategory, which are all short transient scenes.

explanation: Any writing object or performance of writing on any surface. Pens

discriminators: This is object IDENTITY, not morphology. Acoustically it is a short handling / impact / rattle scene indistinguishable from a dozen sibling OBJECTS subcategories — DSP must never assert it. The gates only rule out things that are physically not a handled object (a 10 ms click, a ten-minute bed); the synonyms carry all the real evidence.

Physically a friction texture — pen, pencil or chalk scratching a surface: quiet, bright, sustained, low-crest, with a dense stochastic micro-onset structure. The morphology is measurable; that the implement is a pen rather than a fingernail is not.
It looks like a soft PAPER-FRICTION and will collide with it constantly. It is quieter than almost anything else in the taxonomy, so a very low root_mean_square_level is the one weak hint.

discriminators: The one unmistakable OBJECTS subcategory, and the reason this file is not entirely semantic_only. A zipper is a mechanical comb: every tooth passing the slider is an onset, so the signal is a buzz-rasp with a VERY high onset_rate (tens to hundreds per second) AND a HIGH onset_periodicity. That combination — dense AND periodic — occurs nowhere else in this material/object set.
Every other rasp is stochastic: PAPER-RIP, CLOTH-RIP, PLASTIC-FRICTION and RUBBER-FRICTION are all dense but have onset_periodicity near zero. Periodicity is the entire discriminator.
Zip speed modulates the buzz rate within a single file, so the periodicity prior is deliberately loose on the low side; a slow zip degrades toward a rasp.
Against CLOTH-RIP specifically (UCS sends Velcro there): Velcro is a stochastic tear, a zipper is a periodic buzz.
